16: su ei ho Christos ho huios tou theou tou zontos — you are the Christ the Son of the living God — Peter's confession stacks three titles: Christos (the anointed one = the Messiah), huios tou theou (Son of God = the one in a unique filial relationship to the Father), and tou zontos (of the living God = the God who is distinctively, characteristically alive, contrasted with dead idols). The full Christological confession combines royal Messianism, divine sonship, and the living covenant-God.
18: su ei Petros kai epi taute te petra oikodomeso mou ten ekklesian — you are Peter (Petros) and on this rock (petra) I will build my church — the wordplay is clearer in Aramaic where both are kepha (Cephas). The referent of petra is debated: Peter himself as the founding confessor, Peter's confession of Christ, or Christ himself as the foundational rock (1 Corinthians 10:4). Matthew's context suggests Peter-as-confessor: the one who receives the revelation (Matthew 16:17) is the one on whom the community is built. ekklesia — assembly, congregation — Matthew is the only Gospel to use the word; it translates Hebrew qahal (the covenant assembly of Israel).
1: kai proselthontes hoi Pharisaioi kai Saddoukaioi peirazontes — the Pharisees and Sadducees came, testing him (peirazontes present participle of peirazō: testing with hostile intent — the same word used in 4:1 for Satan's temptation of Jesus) — epērōtēsan auton sēmeion ek tou ouranou epideixai autois (asked him to show them a sign from heaven — ek tou ouranou: from heaven, as distinct from earthly/miraculous signs; they want a cosmic, divine-authentication sign): the Pharisees and Sadducees are normally opponents; their united front signals the gravity of the test. The same demand (with "except the sign of Jonah") appears in Matthew 12:38-40.
2: opsias genomenas legete eudia — evening coming, you say: fair weather (opsias: evening, the late afternoon sky before sunset; eudia: fair weather, clear sky) — pyrrazei gar ho ouranos (for the sky is red/fiery — pyrrazei present of pyrrazō: to be red like fire): the weather-reading examples work by the same logic as the sign-reading Jesus demands of them. Red sky at evening = good weather; red sky in the morning = storm. The observation is accurate meteorology for the Mediterranean.
3: kai prōi sēmeron cheimōn — and in the morning, today stormy (cheimōn: storm, winter weather) — pyrrazei gar stygnazon ho ouranos (for the sky is red and threatening/overcast — stygnazon: dark, gloomy, threatening): the weather-sign contrast establishes the principle. To men prosōpon tou ouranou ginōskete diakrinein (you know how to discern the face of the sky — diakrinein: to distinguish, discern precisely): ta de sēmeia tōn kairōn ou dynasthe (but the signs of the times you cannot — ta sēmeia tōn kairōn: the signs of the appointed moments, the marks of the eschatological season Jesus's ministry inaugurates).
4: genea ponēra kai moichalis sēmeion epizētei — a wicked and adulterous generation seeks a sign — the same verdict as Matthew 12:39. Kai sēmeion ou dothēsetai autē ei mē to sēmeion Iōna (and a sign will not be given except the sign of Jonah — the single exception: the death-and-resurrection sign): kai katalipōn autous apēlthen (and leaving them he went away): the departure is abrupt and complete. The Pharisees and Sadducees receive only the answer they already received in Matthew 12; there is nothing more to say.
5: kai elthontes hoi mathētai eis to peran epelathonto artous labein — coming to the other side the disciples forgot to take bread (epelathonto aorist of epilanthanō: to forget entirely — they had completely forgotten) — the transition from the sign-demand (Matthew 16:4) to the disciples' bread-forgetfulness (Matthew 16:5) sets up the leaven-warning of Matthew 16:6. The disciples' physical bread-anxiety will be contrasted with the spiritual "leaven" warning Jesus is about to deliver; their confusion between literal and metaphorical bread (Matthew 16:7) exposes their still-incomplete understanding.
6: ho de Iēsous eipen autois horate kai prosechete apo tēs zymēs tōn Pharisaiōn kai Saddoukaiōn — Jesus said to them: see and beware of the leaven of the Pharisees and Sadducees (horate kai prosechete: two imperatives — see/look out and be careful; prosechete apo: guard yourselves from, beware of — a warning verb) — zymēs tōn Pharisaiōn kai Saddoukaiōn (the leaven of the Pharisees and Sadducees): Matthew 16:12 decodes this as their teaching, but the disciples initially take it literally because they forgot bread.
7: hoi de dielogizonto en heautois legontes hoti artous ouk elabomen — they were debating among themselves saying that we took no bread (dielogizonto imperfect of dialogizomai: were deliberating, reasoning back and forth — the same internal-reasoning verb as Matthew 9:3-4 where the scribes deliberated about blasphemy) — the disciples assume Jesus is criticizing them for the bread-forgetting rather than warning against a metaphorical contamination. Their literal-mindedness after two miraculous feedings is precisely what Jesus will rebuke in Matthew 16:8-10.
8: gnous de ho Iēsous eipen ti dialogizesthe en heautois oligopistoi — knowing this, Jesus said: why are you deliberating among yourselves, you of little faith (oligopistoi: the coined compound, plural — addressed to the whole group for the first time in this form): hoti artous ouk echete (that you have no bread?): the question is partly rhetorical — Jesus is not confused about what they are discussing but names their deliberation as the product of small faith. The bread-anxiety reveals that they have not drawn the lessons of the two feedings.
9: oupō noeite — do you still not understand (noeite: perceive, apprehend — the intellectual perception verb; oupō: not yet, still not): oude mnēmoneuete tous pente artous tōn pentakischiliōn kai posous kophinous elabete (nor do you remember the five loaves for the five thousand and how many baskets you gathered — kophinous: the Jewish wicker baskets of Matthew 14, named specifically to distinguish from the second feeding's spyridas in Matthew 16:10): Jesus cites the specific basket-type from each feeding, showing the two events are distinct and both are to be remembered.
10: oude tous hepta artous tōn tetrakischiliōn kai posous spyridas elabete — nor the seven loaves for the four thousand and how many baskets you gathered (spyridas: the large hampers of Matthew 15 — Jesus uses the same word Matthew used in Matthew 15:37, confirming these are two distinct events, not one account retold): the juxtaposition of kophinous (Matthew 14) and spyridas (Matthew 15) in Jesus's own summary of both feedings demonstrates that Matthew's use of different basket-words is intentional and carries meaning: two feedings, two communities (Jewish/Gentile contexts suggested), two basket-types.
11: pōs ou noeite hoti ou peri artou eipon hymin — how is it you do not understand that I did not speak to you about bread — the question is a gentle frustration: after two miraculous feedings, anxiety about physical bread is irrational. The disciples have a repeated inability to move from literal to metaphorical when Jesus uses material imagery. Prosechete de apo tēs zymēs tōn Pharisaiōn kai Saddoukaiōn (but beware of the leaven of the Pharisees and Sadducees): the repetition of the warning without decoding it invites the disciples to make the metaphorical move themselves.
12: tote synēkan hoti ouk eipen prosechein apo tēs zymēs tōn artōn — then they understood that he did not say to beware of the leaven of bread (synēkan aorist of syniēmi: they understood at once — the breakthrough understanding that has been building through the chapter) — alla apo tēs didachēs tōn Pharisaiōn kai Saddoukaiōn (but from the teaching of the Pharisees and Sadducees): the decoding reveals that "leaven" = "teaching": pernicious, spreading influence. The sign-demand of Matthew 16:1 and the return-with-no-bread of Matthew 16:5 were both setups for the disciples to learn to read spiritual signs over physical ones.
13: elthōn de ho Iēsous eis ta merē Kaisareias tēs Philippou — coming to the region of Caesarea Philippi (Kaisareia hē Philippou: Caesarea of Philip — the Hellenistic city built by Philip the Tetrarch near Banias/Paneas at the foot of Mount Hermon; a heavily Gentile city with a sanctuary to Pan, hence the ancient name Paneas — the northern limit of Jesus's recorded travels) — ērōta tous mathētas autou legōn tina legousin hoi anthrōpoi einai ton huion tou anthrōpou (he was asking his disciples saying: who do people say the Son of Man is?): the location — far from Jerusalem, in Gentile-associated territory — heightens the confession that follows.
14: hoi de eipan hoi men Iōannēn ton baptistēn — they said: some say John the Baptist — the popular identifications (John the Baptist, Elijah, Jeremiah, one of the prophets) all have in common that they place Jesus within the prophetic tradition rather than exceeding it. Alloi de Ēlian (others Elijah): the Malachi-Elijah expectation (Malachi 4:5). Heteroi de Ieremian ē hena tōn prophētōn (others Jeremiah or one of the prophets): Jeremiah's association with Jesus is unique to Matthew; the tradition of Jeremiah's return may relate to the legend of Jeremiah hiding the temple vessels before the exile.
15: legei autois hymeis de tina me legete einai — he says to them: but who do you say I am (hymeis de: emphatic you — contrasted with "people" of Matthew 16:13; the question now comes directly to the disciples; tina: who, what identity?) — the shift from third-person ("who do people say") to second-person ("who do you say") is the pivot of the entire Gospel to this point. The disciples have traveled with Jesus, received private explanations, witnessed miracles, and received special teaching. Now they must answer for themselves who they believe he is.
17: apokritheis de ho Iēsous eipen autō makarios ei Simōn Bar Iōna — answering, Jesus said to him: blessed are you, Simon son of Jonah (Bar Iōna: Aramaic patronymic — son of Jonah/John; the Aramaic patronymic in a Greek text marks a moment of special personal address) — hoti sarx kai haima ouk apekalypsēn soi (because flesh and blood did not reveal this to you — sarx kai haima: the Semitic idiom for human capacity, human resources, the physical-creaturely realm): alla ho patēr mou ho en tois ouranois (but my Father who is in the heavens): the confession is declared a supernatural disclosure, not a reasoned conclusion reached by human intelligence.
19: dōsō soi tas kleidas tēs basileias tōn ouranōn — I will give you the keys of the kingdom of the heavens (kleidas: keys — the instrument of opening and closing, granting or denying access; in Jewish imagery, the key-holder has administrative authority: the key of David in Isaiah 22:22; Revelation 3:7) — kai ho ean dēsēs epi tēs gēs estai dedemenon en tois ouranois (and whatever you bind on earth will be bound in heaven — dedemenon perfect passive: will be in a state of having been bound — the perfect passive in heaven may indicate that heaven ratifies the earthly binding; the rabbinic formula for binding/loosing was used for prohibiting/permitting halakhic rulings, not just for church discipline): kai ho ean lysēs epi tēs gēs estai lelumenon en tois ouranois — and whatever you loose on earth will be loosed in heaven.
20: tote diesteilato tois mathētais hina mēdeni eipōsin hoti autos estin ho Christos — then he charged the disciples that they should tell no one that he is the Messiah (diesteilato aorist of diastellomai: to give explicit orders, to charge strictly) — the messianic secret following Peter's confession is not denial of the title but management of its timing and interpretation. The disciples must not announce the Messiahship before its meaning has been defined by the passion (Matthew 16:21). An uninterpreted "Messiah" announcement would be misunderstood in political terms; the passion must first reframe what "Messiah" means.
21: apo tote ērxato ho Iēsous deiknynai tois mathētais autou — from that time Jesus began to show his disciples (apo tote ērxato: from that time he began — one of Matthew's structural markers; the first "from that time" is 4:17 at the start of the Galilean ministry; this second marks a new phase: the passion-instruction phase) — hoti dei auton eis Ierosolyma apelthein (that it is necessary for him to go to Jerusalem — dei: it is necessary, divinely necessary — not compelled externally but internally required by the divine plan): the first passion prediction names Jerusalem, suffering by elders/chief priests/scribes, death, and resurrection on the third day.
22: kai proslabomenos auton ho Petros ērxato epitiman autō legōn — taking him aside Peter began to rebuke him saying (proslabomenos: taking aside — Peter separates Jesus from the others for the private rebuke; ērxato epitiman: began to rebuke — epitimaō, the rebuke-verb used for silencing demons and rebuking wind/waves) — ileos soi kyrie (God be merciful to you, Lord — ileos soi: may God be gracious/merciful to you; not a mere interjection but an invocation of divine protection): ou mē estai soi touto (this will never happen to you — the strongest possible negation).
23: ho de strapheis tō Petrō eipen hypage opisō mou Satana — turning to Peter he said: get behind me, Satan (strapheis: turning — a deliberate physical reorientation; hypage opisō mou: go behind me — the discipleship-command reversed: Peter who was called to follow-behind-Jesus is now told to return to behind-Jesus, away from in-front-of-Jesus where he was blocking the path) — skandalon ei emou (you are a stumbling-block to me — skandalon: a trap, an obstacle, a stumbling-block — placed in Jesus's path): hoti ou phroneis ta tou theou alla ta tōn anthrōpōn (for you are minding the things of God, but the things of men — phroneis: the present tense of phroneō, to set the mind on, to be oriented toward).
24: tote ho Iēsous eipen tois mathētais autou ei tis thelei opisō mou elthein — then Jesus said to his disciples: if anyone wants to come after me (opisō mou elthein: to come behind me — the standard discipleship formula) — aparnēsasthō heauton kai aratō ton stauron autou kai akoloutheitō moi (let him deny himself and take up his cross and follow me): three aorist imperatives forming the discipleship-definition: self-denial (aparnēsasthō: to deny completely, disown oneself — the same verb as Peter's denial in Matthew 26:70,72); cross-taking (aratō ton stauron autou: take up his cross — the specific instrument of execution; cf. Matthew 10:38); following (akoloutheitō present imperative: ongoing following).
25: hos gar ean thelē tēn psychēn autou sōsai apolesei autēn — for whoever wants to save his life will lose it (psychēn: life/soul — the word spanning physical survival, personal identity, and the whole self; sōsai: to save, preserve; apolesei future: will lose, will destroy) — hos d an apolese tēn psychēn autou heneken emou heurēsei autēn (but whoever loses his life for my sake will find it — heneken emou: for my sake, on account of me — the criterion): the paradox operates on psychē: protecting the self-life destroys it; surrendering it to Jesus finds the true life. Cf. 10:39 where the same saying appears in the mission discourse.
26: ti gar ōphelēthēsetai anthrōpos ean ton kosmon holon kerdēsē — for what will it profit a person if he gains the whole world (ōphelēthēsetai future passive of ōpheleō: what benefit will accrue; kerdēsē aorist subjunctive of kerdainō: to gain, to profit financially) — tēn de psychēn autou zēmiōthē (but loses his soul — zēmiōthē aorist passive of zēmioō: to suffer loss, to be penalized, to forfeit — a commercial/legal term for incurring a loss): ē ti dōsei anthrōpos antallagna tēs psychēs autou (or what will a person give in exchange for his soul — antallagna: a counter-exchange, the price paid to recover something surrendered).
27: mellei gar ho huios tou anthrōpou erchesthai en tē doxē tou patros autou meta tōn angelōn autou — for the Son of Man is about to come in the glory of his Father with his angels (mellei: is about to, is going to — the verb of imminent futurity; en tē doxē tou patros autou: in the glory of his Father — the eschatological coming in divine glory) — kai tote apodōsei hekastō kata tēn praxin autou (and then he will repay each one according to his practice/actions — praxin: action, practice, deed — the Judgment involves what one habitually does, not merely a single act): the ground for the cross-bearing saying (Matthew 16:24-26) is the coming judgment.
28: amen legō hymin hoti eisin tines tōn hōde hestōtōn — truly I tell you there are some of those standing here (tōn hōde hestōtōn: those standing here — a specific reference to the physical persons present) — oitines ou mē geusōntai thanatou heōs an idōsin ton huion tou anthrōpou erchomenon en tē basileia autou (who will not taste death until they see the Son of Man coming in his kingdom — geusōntai thanatou: taste death, a Semitic idiom for experiencing death; en tē basileia autou: in his kingdom): the saying has generated sustained debate — interpretations include the Transfiguration (immediately following in Matthew 17:1-8), Pentecost, the destruction of Jerusalem, or the parousia.
